metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Foundations of Mathematics 11 develops students' reasoning, proof, and geometric thinking alongside statistical analysis, quadratic functions, and financial literacy. This course is designed for students pursuing post-secondary programs that do not require Pre-calculus, including social sciences, business, healthcare, and arts. It develops mathematical reasoning, logical argumentation, and applied statistical skills — preparing students for Foundations of Mathematics 12.
metadata.general_description: A senior mathematics course developing logical reasoning, deductive proof, angle geometry, graphical analysis of quadratic and linear functions, optimization, statistical analysis, and financial literacy — supporting post-secondary pathways in social sciences, business, and health.

big_ideas.statement: Similar shapes and objects have proportional relationships that can be described, measured, and compared.
big_ideas.elaboration: Similarity — the foundation of trigonometry and scale — underpins the measurement of inaccessible distances and the design of scale models.

big_ideas.statement: Optimization informs the decision-making process in situations involving extreme values.
big_ideas.elaboration: Maximizing or minimizing quantities — area, profit, cost, volume — within constraints is a powerful application of quadratic functions and linear programming with broad real-world relevance.

big_ideas.statement: Logical reasoning helps us discover and describe mathematical truths.
big_ideas.elaboration: Inductive and deductive reasoning, formal proof, and logical argumentation are the tools through which mathematical knowledge is established — and through which students develop critical thinking.

big_ideas.statement: Statistical analysis allows us to notice, wonder about, and answer questions about variation.
big_ideas.elaboration: Understanding how data varies — through standard deviation, distributions, z-scores, and confidence intervals — allows us to make statistically valid claims and evaluate the reliability of research.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and play games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible, and strategic thinking; model with mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively and with curiosity when exploring problems.
i_can_statements: I can develop and apply logical reasoning strategies to mathematical proofs and problems.
I can use technology (Desmos, spreadsheets) to explore quadratic functions and statistical data.
I can model optimization problems using linear inequalities and quadratic functions.
I can think creatively about feasible solutions within constraints.

competencies.raw: Develop mathematical understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore and illustrate concepts; apply flexible and strategic approaches; solve with persistence and positive disposition; engage in problem-solving connected with place, story, and cultural practices.
i_can_statements: I can develop understanding of logical reasoning, statistics, and optimization through inquiry.
I can visualize quadratic functions, feasibility regions, and statistical distributions.
I can apply flexible algebraic and graphical approaches to solve optimization and analysis problems.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary in discussions; take risks when offering ideas.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify mathematical proofs and statistical conclusions.
I can represent mathematical ideas using graphs, diagrams, equations, and written arguments.
I can use statistical and algebraic vocabulary accurately in discussions.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect concepts with each other and personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, knowledge, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my logical reasoning and statistical thinking and identify areas for growth.
I can connect mathematics to social justice, civic reasoning, and real-world decision-making.
I can incorporate First Peoples mathematical perspectives, including traditional measurement and spatial knowledge.

areas.name: Mathematical Reasoning and Proof
areas.raw: Logical reasoning: conjecturing, inductive and deductive reasoning, proofs, puzzles.
topics: Inductive reasoning: making conjectures from patterns; identifying counterexamples
Deductive reasoning: logical arguments, if-then statements, contrapositive, inverse, converse
Formal geometric proof: two-column and paragraph proofs
Analyzing puzzles and games: applying logical reasoning systematically

areas.name: Angle Relationships and Geometry
topics: Angle properties: complementary, supplementary, vertically opposite, linear pair
Parallel lines and transversals: corresponding, alternate interior/exterior, co-interior angles
Triangle angle properties: sum of interior angles, exterior angle theorem
Polygon angle sum formulas: interior and exterior angles
Geometric constructions: perpendicular bisector, angle bisector, tangent lines
Circle properties: central and inscribed angles, tangent-chord angles

areas.name: Graphical Analysis and Optimization
areas.raw: Linear inequalities, quadratic functions, systems, and optimization.
topics: Graphing linear inequalities in two variables: shading feasible regions
Systems of inequalities: graphing and identifying feasible regions
Quadratic functions: vertex form, standard form, factored form; transformations
Characteristics of quadratic functions: vertex, axis of symmetry, max/min, domain, range
Systems of equations: linear-linear, linear-quadratic, graphically and algebraically
Optimization: maximizing/minimizing an objective function within constraints

areas.name: Statistics
areas.raw: Statistical analysis: data collection, central tendency, standard deviation, z-scores, confidence intervals.
topics: Data collection: survey design, sampling methods, and potential bias
Measures of central tendency: mean, median, mode
Measures of spread: range, variance, standard deviation
Normal distribution: properties, symmetry, and the 68-95-99.7 rule
Z-scores: calculating and interpreting standardized scores
Confidence intervals: estimating population parameters from sample data

areas.name: Scale Models and Similarity
areas.raw: Scale models: enlargements, reductions, property comparisons, square-cube law.
topics: Similar polygons: proportional sides and equal angles
Scale factors in 2D: area changes with square of scale factor
Scale factors in 3D: volume changes with cube of scale factor (square-cube law)
Applications: architecture, engineering models, dosage calculations, and biology

areas.name: Financial Literacy
areas.raw: Financial literacy: compound interest, investments, loans, buy/lease decisions.
topics: Compound interest formula: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt)
Comparing simple and compound interest over time
Investments: GICs, RRSPs, and comparing growth options
Loans: total cost of borrowing, monthly payments, and amortization
Buy vs. lease analysis: calculating total costs and making informed decisions
